title: Polymer clay mokume-gane pendant
subtitle: A multi-layer polymer clay pendant with woodgrain patterning
excerpt: Mokume-gane is a Japanese metalwork technique adapted for polymer clay: layers of different colours are pressed together, impressed from underneath, and then sliced thinly across the top to reveal complex swirling patterns. This tutorial makes one large pendant.
sourceNotes: Mokume-gane is a Japanese mixed-metal technique traditionally applied to sword fittings. The polymer clay adaptation is widely taught in craft jewellery. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A Japanese metalwork technique meaning 'woodgrain metal'. In polymer clay, layers of different colours are distorted from underneath and sliced to reveal complex wood-like patterns.
A PVC-based modelling material that cures in a domestic oven at 110-130°C.
Oven-baking polymer clay to harden it permanently.

content.text: produces unique swirling patterns that are different in every piece. The build stacks thin sheets of contrasting colours, distorts them by pushing objects up from underneath, and then slices across the distorted surface to reveal the cross-section. The result resembles wood grain or agate banding.

attrs.content: Use a dedicated oven tray. Do not use the same tray for food after curing polymer clay. Cure at the manufacturer's stated temperature; burning polymer clay releases fumes.

items.name: Rounded object for distortion (pencil eraser, marble, button)

content.text: Condition each colour thoroughly. Roll each colour to a sheet 2 mm thick and cut to a uniform size (approximately 80 mm x 60 mm). Stack the sheets on top of each other, alternating colours. Press the stack firmly together with the rolling pin, rolling gently once across the full stack to bond the layers without distorting their boundaries.

Turn the stack upside down. Press the rounded ends of pencils, marbles, or large buttons firmly into the underside of the stack from below, pushing up 10-15 mm. The impressions create domes in the stack. Vary the position, size, and depth of the impressions across the whole surface. Each depression from below will produce a concentric ring pattern when the top is sliced.

Turn the stack right side up. Place the fettling knife or craft knife blade flat and horizontal at the level of the dome tops, roughly 2-3 mm into the top surface. Take a single thin slice across the full surface in one smooth stroke, cutting through the distorted tops of the domes. Set aside the slice for use on the pendant. Look at the sliced surface: each dome produces a set of concentric colour rings where the blade crossed it. Take a second slice from the same surface for additional pieces.

Roll a small sheet of a neutral colour (white, cream, or the dominant colour of your palette) to 4 mm thick. Cut a round, oval, or rectangular pendant base approximately 40 mm across. Lay the patterned slice over the pendant base and press it flat. Roll gently once with the rolling pin to fully adhere the slice to the base.
Press a bail finding into the top edge of the pendant, or pierce a hole with the needle tool for a jump ring. Preheat the oven to 130°C.
for 20 minutes on a dedicated tray. Cool fully before handling or sanding.

Sand the cured pendant with 400-grit wet-and-dry sandpaper under running water to smooth any edges or uneven slicing marks. Progress to 800 grit, then 1500 grit for a polished surface. Buff dry with a cloth. The sanded polymer clay surface has a natural low sheen that is more refined than varnish.
Use metallic gold and black clay as two of the layers for a convincing metal-inlay appearance.
Cut the pendant into a free organic shape after slicing, to show off the flowing patterns.
Make a matching set of three pieces using slices from the same stack for visual consistency.

items.cause: Layers were not fully bonded before distortion
items.fix: Roll the assembled stack more firmly before distorting, using a few passes rather than one

items.symptom: Pattern looks muddy with no clear rings
items.cause: Too many similar colours stacked; the slices do not show enough contrast
items.fix: Use at least three strongly contrasting colours. A dark and a light colour always in the stack provide readable ring edges

items.cause: Clay not fully conditioned, or knife blade not thin and sharp enough
items.fix: Recondition any clay that feels crumbly before building. A craft knife gives cleaner slices than a thick clay blade for thin slice work
